title: Arts and Culture: Music
overview: Music is an important part of British culture, with a rich and varied heritage ranging from classical music to modern pop. The UK has produced world-renowned composers from Henry Purcell to Benjamin Britten, and revolutionary popular music from The Beatles to modern artists. This lesson covers classical composers, The Proms, popular music history, major venues, festivals, and music awards.
key_facts: The Proms is an eight-week summer season of orchestral classical music, organised by the BBC since 1927, held at the Royal Albert Hall and other venues.
The Last Night of the Proms is the most well-known concert and is broadcast on television.
Henry Purcell (1659-95) was organist at Westminster Abbey and developed a distinct British musical style.
George Frideric Handel (1695-1759), German-born, became a British citizen in 1727 and wrote Water Music and Music for the Royal Fireworks.
Handel wrote the oratorio Messiah, sung regularly by choirs, often at Easter.
Gustav Holst (1874-1934) composed The Planets suite and adapted Jupiter as the tune for 'I vow to thee my country'.
Sir Edward Elgar (1857-1934) composed the Pomp and Circumstance Marches; March No 1 (Land of Hope and Glory) is played at the Last Night of the Proms.
Ralph Vaughan Williams (1872-1958) was strongly influenced by traditional English folk music.
Sir William Walton (1902-83) wrote marches for the coronations of King George VI and Queen Elizabeth II; best-known works are Façade and Balthazar's Feast.
Benjamin Britten (1913-76) is best known for operas Peter Grimes and Billy Budd, and A Young Person's Guide to the Orchestra.
Britten founded the Aldeburgh festival in Suffolk, a popular international music event.
Since the 1960s, British pop music has been culturally significant; The Beatles and The Rolling Stones remain influential worldwide.
British pop has innovated through movements like Punk (late 1970s) and boy/girl bands (1990s).
Major music venues include Wembley Stadium, The O2 in Greenwich, and the SECC in Glasgow.
Famous summer festivals include Glastonbury, Isle of Wight Festival, and V Festival.
The National Eisteddfod of Wales is an annual cultural festival with music, dance, art, and Welsh poetry competitions.
The Mercury Music Prize is awarded each September for the best album from the UK and Ireland.
The Brit Awards is an annual event with categories like best British group and best British solo artist.
memory_hook: Remember British music chronologically: Purcell (1600s) sets British style → Handel arrives and becomes British, writes Water Music for river parties → Victorian era brings Elgar's patriotic Land of Hope and Glory → 1900s: Holst's Planets, Britten's operas and Aldeburgh festival → 1960s EXPLOSION: Beatles and Stones conquer world → Summer festivals (Glastonbury!) and awards (Mercury Prize, Brit Awards) celebrate ongoing innovation.

options: An eight-week summer season of orchestral classical music organised by the BBC
questions.explanation: The Proms is an eight-week summer season of orchestral classical music held at various venues including the Royal Albert Hall, organised by the BBC since 1927.

questions.prompt: Which composer became a British citizen in 1727?
questions.explanation: George Frideric Handel, German-born, became a British citizen in 1727. He composed Water Music and Music for the Royal Fireworks.
Gustav Holst composed The Planets, a suite themed around the planets. He adapted Jupiter as the tune for 'I vow to thee my country'.

questions.prompt: Which piece is played at the Last Night of the Proms?
questions.explanation: Sir Edward Elgar's Pomp and Circumstance March No 1 (Land of Hope and Glory) is usually played at the Last Night of the Proms.
Benjamin Britten founded the Aldeburgh festival in Suffolk, which continues to be a popular music event of international importance.

questions.prompt: Which bands have been influential since the 1960s?
options: The Beatles and The Rolling Stones
questions.explanation: The Beatles and The Rolling Stones have been influential since the 1960s, continuing to impact music both in the UK and abroad.

questions.prompt: What is the Mercury Music Prize awarded for?
options: Best album from the UK and Ireland
questions.explanation: The Mercury Music Prize is awarded each September for the best album from the UK and Ireland.

questions.prompt: Name famous UK summer music festivals.
options: Glastonbury, Isle of Wight, V Festival
questions.explanation: Famous UK summer festivals include Glastonbury, the Isle of Wight Festival and the V Festival.

questions.prompt: What is the National Eisteddfod of Wales?
options: An annual cultural festival with music, dance, art and Welsh poetry
questions.explanation: The National Eisteddfod of Wales is an annual cultural festival with music, dance, art and performances largely in Welsh, including Welsh poetry competitions.

questions.prompt: Who was influenced by English folk music?
questions.explanation: Ralph Vaughan Williams wrote music for orchestras and choirs and was strongly influenced by traditional English folk music.
